comments:
Summary:
This paper studies PAC learning when labels are obtained from a crowd of labelers that may be noisy or adversarial. The authors propose algorithms that interleave labeling and learning (rather than first obtaining high-quality labels and then learning), using a combination of probabilistic filtering, boosting-style composition, and a pruning procedure that uses a small number of golden queries. The main positive result is that when a constant fraction α of the labelers are perfect, any hypothesis class F that is efficiently learnable in the realizable PAC model can still be learned efficiently from the crowd with only O(1) label queries per example on average and O(1) load per labeler (with dependence on α when α is small). The paper also treats the case where no perfect labelers exist and shows how to identify all good labelers when a majority are good. The work provides theoretical guarantees, lemmas, and sketches/proofs, and situates itself against related work in crowdsourcing, noise-tolerant learning, and active learning.

Strengths:
- The paper addresses an important and timely question: how to exploit the structure of a crowd to obtain computationally efficient PAC learning with low labeling overhead. This directly targets practical issues in crowdsourcing pipelines.
- The main idea—interleaving learning and adaptive querying using probabilistic filtering and boosting-inspired composition—is novel and well motivated.
- Theoretical results are comprehensive: the authors provide algorithms for the strong-majority case (α > 1/2 + const), extend to general α using a small number of golden queries, and analyze detection of good labelers when no perfect ones exist. The results include bounds on total query complexity, load per labeler, and sample requirements relative to the standard PAC sample complexity.
- The paper places the contribution relative to a broad range of related work (Dekel & Shamir, Karger et al., active learning literature, etc.) and clarifies distinctions.

Weaknesses / Limitations:
- Model assumptions and practicality: the strongest guarantees rely on the realizable setting (existence of f* in F) and a noticeable fraction of perfect labelers. While common in theory, these assumptions may limit direct applicability to many real-world crowdsourcing scenarios where labelers are imperfect and the realizable assumption is violated.
- Golden queries: the approach for small α requires access to a small number of golden queries (trusted labels). While the authors discuss this and quantify the needed number (O(1/α)), reliance on such an oracle may be a practical hurdle; it would help to discuss realistic mechanisms for obtaining these golden labels and the sensitivity when these golden labels are themselves noisy.
- Some proofs and technical steps are presented at a sketch level or deferred to appendices; certain constants and poly(·) dependencies are omitted or hidden in big-O notation. Although the lemmas appear plausible and standard probabilistic tools are used, more explicit clarification of constant factors and clearer statements of the trade-offs (e.g., when the per-example cost truly becomes O(1) in concrete parameter regimes) would strengthen the presentation.
- No empirical evaluation: the paper is purely theoretical. Empirical simulations or experiments on synthetic or real crowdsourced data would help illustrate the practical behavior (constants, overheads, sensitivity to α, and load balancing among labelers).
- Adversarial behaviours and collusion: while the model allows arbitrary behavior by bad labelers, the analysis sometimes relies on pruning procedures and independence sampling; discussion of worst-case collusion or labeler correlations (e.g., many bad labelers sharing identical but adversarial functions) and their effect on the algorithms would be useful.

Suggestions for improvement:
- Add a short experimental section (even synthetic) to validate the claimed O(1) average cost per labeled example, illustrate dependence on α, and show the actual constants in practice.
- Expand discussion on obtaining golden queries (practicality, costs) and robustness when golden labels are noisy.
- Clarify and perhaps tighten some of the technical constants and parameter regimes where the asymptotic statements translate to small constants in practice.
- Discuss extensions or possible relaxations: moving beyond realizability (agnostic case), handling continuous labeler quality spectrum, heterogenous costs/prices for labelers, or adaptive mechanisms for when labelers may abstain.

Overall assessment and recommendation:
This is a strong, original theoretical contribution that addresses an important question at the intersection of learning theory and crowdsourcing. The techniques are novel and the results advance understanding of how to leverage crowd structure to achieve computationally efficient PAC learning with low labeling overhead. I recommend acceptance; the work will be of interest to the learning theory and crowdsourcing communities. My confidence is reasonably high given the detailed lemmas and proofs provided, though an empirical demonstration and discussion of practical aspects would further increase the impact.
